content.problemStatement:
An array is called interesting if no subarray of length **greater than** 222 is non-increasing or non-decreasing.

Chef has an array AAA of length NNN. He wants to make the array **interesting** by rearranging the elements in any order.

If there exist multiple such arrays, output any one.

If no such array exists, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN denoting the length of array AAA.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​




Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line, any possible **interesting** array.
If no such array is possible, output −1-1−1.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line. If it is possible to reorder the given numbers into an interesting array, print any such ordering as N space‑separated integers. If it is impossible, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: All elements are equal, frequency 3 > floor(3/2)=1, so impossible.\nTest case 2: Sorted array is [2,2,5,6]; interleaving gives [5,2,6,2] which has no monotone subarray of length ≥3.\nTest case 3: Sorted array is [2,2,4,5,5]; interleaving gives [4,2,5,2,5] which satisfies the condition.

solution.approach: An array is interesting iff no subarray of length ≥ 3 is monotone (non‑decreasing or non‑increasing). This is equivalent to requiring that every three consecutive elements are not monotone. If a value appears more than ⌊N/2⌋ times, any arrangement will contain three equal (or monotone) elements, so the answer is impossible. Otherwise we can always build a valid ordering: sort the array, split it into a lower half L (first ⌊N/2⌋ elements) and an upper half R (the remaining elements). Then output elements by alternating R[i] followed by L[i] for i = 0 … |L|‑1, and finally the last element of R if N is odd. This interleaving guarantees that any three consecutive elements consist of two from R and one from L or vice‑versa, which cannot be monotone because every element of R is ≥ every element of L and the strict inequality holds for at least one pair (otherwise the frequency condition would be violated). The construction runs in O(N log N) per test case due to sorting.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting dominates).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the sorted array and the result.
